skill.name: sympy
skill.description: Guides symbolic mathematics work with SymPy, including algebra, calculus, equation solving, matrices, physics formulas, exact arithmetic, and mathematical code generation.
skill.summary: Use SymPy for exact symbolic math, equation solving, calculus, matrices, physics, and code generation.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review found no malicious intent or prompt injection attempts. Most static findings are false positives from markdown backticks, Python import examples, and mathematical terms, but the skill does include eval reconstruction guidance, generated code, file writes, and autowrap compilation examples that require user caution.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe eval Reconstruction Guidance
medium_findings.description: The documentation states that a SymPy string representation can be eval()ed to recreate an expression. This is legitimate SymPy documentation, but it can become code execution if users apply it to untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The eval() reference is explicit and semantically related to expression reconstruction. Confidence is not higher because the skill does not execute eval itself and later warns users to validate parsed input.

medium_findings.title: Generated Native Code and Compilation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches generation of C and Fortran code and autowrap compilation into Python-callable functions. This is expected for SymPy code generation, but generated native code and compiler toolchains should be reviewed before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code generation and compilation examples are clear and intentional. They appear educational rather than malicious, so the concern is operational risk instead of confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The large set of Ruby or shell backtick detections comes from markdown inline code and fenced Python examples. The reviewed locations show mathematical snippets, imports, and explanatory text rather than shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative scanner locations are markdown code spans or fenced Python snippets. No shell command execution syntax or instruction to run shell commands was found in these examples.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import and Function Constructor Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alerts correspond to normal Python import statements split across lines. The Function constructor alert is SymPy Function usage, not the JavaScript dynamic Function constructor.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static Python imports and SymPy API examples. They do not dynamically load attacker-controlled modules or construct executable code from strings.

low_findings.title: Cryptography, Certificate, C2, and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, certificate or key, C2 keyword, and reconnaissance alerts match mathematical or documentation terms. Examples include C2 as an integration constant, eigenvals().keys(), and valid C output text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review shows benign mathematical context at the cited locations. No credential material, C2 behavior, system probing, or cryptographic implementation was found there.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The network findings are static links to official SymPy documentation and the SymPy examples repository. They are not runtime network calls and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain markdown resource links. There is no fetch, socket, webhook, telemetry, or credential exfiltration behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: eval() Mention for Expression Rehydration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill mentions eval() as a way to recreate a SymPy expression from its representation. Use only trusted expressions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The eval() guidance is explicit, but it appears in reference documentation and is not executed by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Write Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that write LaTeX, text, Python, and generated C output files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly open files in write mode. They use local fixed filenames or generated names, with no evidence of credential access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Compilation Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The autowrap example compiles generated C or Fortran code and imports it as a Python callable.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The compile-and-import behavior is directly described. It is legitimate SymPy functionality but should be used with reviewed expressions and trusted toolchains.

content.value_statement: Symbolic math work often needs exact formulas, not rounded numerical results. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through SymPy workflows for algebra, calculus, matrices, physics, and code generation.
actual_capabilities: Create symbols, assumptions, and exact arithmetic expressions in SymPy.
Solve algebraic equations, systems, and differential equations with SymPy solvers.
Compute derivatives, integrals, limits, series, and simplifications.
Work with symbolic matrices, eigenvalues, determinants, and linear systems.
Use SymPy physics modules for mechanics, units, optics, and quantum examples.
Generate LaTeX, Python-callable functions, and C or Fortran output from expressions.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not install SymPy or verify a local Python environment.
Some symbolic problems may not have closed-form solutions.
Generated code and compiled wrappers must be reviewed before execution.
Large symbolic expressions can be slow or memory intensive.

use_cases.title: Check Algebra and Calculus Work
use_cases.description: Derive exact formulas, simplify expressions, and verify derivatives or integrals before using numerical methods.

use_cases.title: Prototype Scientific Models
use_cases.description: Represent equations symbolically, solve systems, and convert formulas into fast numerical functions.

use_cases.title: Generate Mathematical Output
use_cases.description: Turn expressions into LaTeX, Python-callable functions, or generated source code for technical workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Simplify an Expression
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the SymPy skill to simplify this expression exactly. Explain the assumptions I should define first: [expression].

prompt_templates.title: Solve Equations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SymPy to solve this equation or system symbolically. Show the recommended solver and interpret the solution set: [equations].

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a Matrix
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SymPy to analyze this symbolic matrix. Find determinant, inverse conditions, eigenvalues, and any useful simplifications: [matrix].

prompt_templates.title: Generate Reviewed Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use SymPy to convert this formula into executable numerical code. Include safety checks before writing files or compiling output: [formula].

output_examples.input: Simplify a trigonometric identity involving sine and cosine.
output_examples.output: The response identifies the needed symbols, applies a SymPy simplification strategy, and returns the exact identity result.

output_examples.input: Solve a two-variable linear system with symbolic coefficients.
output_examples.output: The response selects a linear solver, describes parameter conditions, and explains when a unique solution exists.

output_examples.input: Prepare a formula for fast numerical evaluation.
output_examples.output: The response recommends a lambdified function, lists required imports, and warns to review generated or compiled code.

best_practices: Define symbols and assumptions before building expressions.
Prefer exact numbers such as Rational values when exact results matter.
Validate untrusted input before parsing, evaluating, writing, or compiling generated output.
anti_patterns: Do not use eval() on expressions from untrusted users or files.
Do not use floating-point literals when exact symbolic arithmetic is required.
Do not compile or run generated code without reviewing the expression and output files.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant use SymPy for exact symbolic mathematics in Python.

faq.question: Can it solve every equation exactly?
faq.answer: No. Some equations have no closed-form solution or require numerical methods.

faq.question: Does it run SymPy by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. A working Python environment must run the code.

faq.question: Is generated code safe to execute?
faq.answer: Generated code should be reviewed first, especially before writing files or compiling native extensions.

faq.question: Can it create LaTeX output?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include SymPy printing workflows for LaTeX and other text formats.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
